chunk_summary: Guidelines for monitoring and stop rules during inpatient stabilization of SAM, focusing on hydration, feeding, and failure-to-respond triggers within the first 10 days of care.
learning_objectives: Understand the monitoring frequencies and stop rules for IV and oral/NG rehydration in SAM management.
Identify failure-to-respond timepoints and triggers for diagnostic reassessment in SAM care.
Learn the criteria for escalating feeding support and tracking oedema resolution.
Recognize signs of overhydration and complications during early inpatient care.
key_facts: Monitor pulse and respiratory rate every 5–10 minutes during IV fluid administration; stop if pulse rises by 15/min or respiratory rate rises by 5/min.
Reassess hydration every 30 minutes for the first 2 hours, then hourly during oral/NG rehydration; stop if tachypnea, rising pulse, or periorbital puffiness develops.
Weigh daily and track oedema grade until resolution; failure to reduce oedema by day 4 or persistence to day 10 requires reassessment.
Failure to gain ≥5 g/kg/day for three consecutive days after transition to free feeding is a trigger for further evaluation.
Reassess for missed infections, electrolyte issues, or comorbidities if appetite has not returned by day 4.
reference_quote: For any child receiving IV fluids for shock, Indian ward thresholds operationalize safety: record pulse and respiratory rate at the start and at least every 5–10 minutes, and stop the infusion immediately if respiratory rate rises by 5/min or pulse rises by 15/min.
document_key_topics: Monitoring and stop rules for hydration in SAM care
Feeding support and oedema tracking in inpatient SAM management
